label: The Twelve — Part 2 — Yoel and Amos
summary_plain: Yoel describes a devastating locust invasion — the land stripped bare, the grain offering and drink offering cut off from the house of YHWH. Blow the shofar in Tziyon! YHWH promises to pour out his ruach on all flesh — your sons and daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, your young men shall see visions. Even on the male and female servants I will pour out my ruach. YHWH will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Yehoshafat and enter into judgment with them there on account of my people Yisra'EL. Amos — a herdsman from Teqoa — declares YHWH's roar from Tziyon against the nations. For three transgressions and for four. YHWH demands justice: let justice roll down like waters and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream. You who turn justice to wormwood and cast down righteousness to the earth. YHWH says: I hate, I despise your festivals — I take no delight in your solemn assemblies. But let justice roll down.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the Minor Prophets Scroll (4QXII, 8HevXIIgr, and Murabba'at) and the Orit Ge'ez. Yoel's ruach-pouring prophecy (2:28-29/3:1-2 in the Hebrew) is among the most significant in the prophetic literature — YHWH's ruach poured out on all flesh regardless of gender or social status, including servants. Acts 2 cites this passage at Pentecost, but the original text promises prophetic empowerment to all covenant people — not the establishment of a new institution. Amos is the foundational social justice prophet. His background — a herdsman, not a professional prophet (7:14) — establishes that prophetic authority comes from YHWH's call, not institutional training. The judgment formula 'for three transgressions and for four' covers Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moav, Yehudah, and Yisra'EL — YHWH judges all nations by the same standard of justice.

key_terms.definition: Prophet whose name means 'YHWH is Creator.' He prophesies the outpouring of YHWH's ruach on all flesh and the gathering of nations for judgment in the Valley of Yehoshafat. Anglicized as Joel.

A herdsman and dresser of sycamore figs from Teqoa. Not a professional prophet nor a prophet's son (7:14). YHWH took him from following the flock and sent him to prophesy. He demands justice and righteousness — and condemns the wealthy who oppress the poor.

Yoel 2:28-29 (3:1-2 in Hebrew numbering): YHWH will pour out his ruach (spirit/breath/wind) on all flesh — sons, daughters, old men, young men, male servants, female servants. No gender or class restriction on prophetic empowerment.

Yoel 3:2 (4:2 in Hebrew): YHWH will gather all nations and bring them down to the Valley of Yehoshafat for judgment. The name means 'YHWH judges.' This is the location of the final reckoning between YHWH and the nations that scattered his people.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH declare about the nations in the Valley of Yehoshafat?
options: He will bless them and forgive them
He will gather them for judgment on account of his people Yisra'EL
He will make a covenant with them
He will scatter them as he scattered Yisra'EL
multiple_choice.source_quote: I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Yehoshafat, and I will enter into judgment with them there on account of my people and my heritage Yisra'EL.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH say about the festivals and solemn assemblies?
options: He delights in them above all else
He hates and despises them — he demands justice and righteousness instead
He will attend them in person
multiple_choice.source_quote: I hate, I despise your festivals, and I take no delight in your solemn assemblies. But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.

multiple_choice.question: What was Amos before YHWH called him?
options: A herdsman and dresser of sycamore figs
multiple_choice.source_quote: I was no prophet, nor a prophet's son, but I was a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore figs. And YHWH took me from following the flock.

faq.question: How did the Great Eraser domesticate Amos's demand for justice?
faq.answer: Amos 5:24 — 'let justice roll down like waters and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream' — is quoted routinely in Western culture, stripped of its context. The full passage (5:21-24) begins: 'I hate, I despise your festivals. I take no delight in your solemn assemblies. Even though you offer me your burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them. Take away from me the noise of your songs.' YHWH is not requesting justice as an addition to religious practice — he is rejecting religious practice that exists without justice. The Great Eraser turned Amos into a motivational quote on church walls while the churches themselves participated in slavery, Jim Crow, colonialism, and wealth extraction from the covenant people. Amos was a herdsman — not seminary-trained, not institutionally authorized. YHWH took him from following the flock and sent him to confront the religious establishment. The institutional religious system he confronted in the 8th century BCE is structurally identical to the one that quotes him while perpetuating injustice.

faq.question: What does Yoel's ruach prophecy mean for the covenant community?
faq.answer: Yoel 2:28-29 (3:1-2 in Hebrew numbering) promises the outpouring of YHWH's ruach on all flesh — explicitly including sons, daughters, old men, young men, male servants, and female servants. No gender exclusion. No class exclusion. No institutional gatekeeping. Acts 2 claims the fulfillment of this passage at Pentecost — but immediately thereafter, the institutional Church built a hierarchy that excluded women from leadership and denied servants prophetic authority. The promise is YHWH's ruach on ALL flesh. The Great Eraser restricted prophetic authority to ordained male clergy within institutional structures — the exact opposite of what Yoel prophesied. The restoration of the covenant community requires the restoration of what Yoel described: prophetic empowerment without institutional permission, without gender restriction, without class barriers.

faq.question: Why does YHWH gather the nations for judgment specifically 'on account of my people Yisra'EL'?
faq.answer: Yoel 3:2 (4:2): 'I will enter into judgment with them there on account of my people and my heritage Yisra'EL, whom they have scattered among the nations. They have divided up my land.' The judgment of the nations is not abstract — it is specifically for the scattering of Yisra'EL and the division of the land. Every empire that participated in the scattering — Assyria, Babylon, Rome, the Arab slave trade, the European slave trade, the colonial partition of Africa — is subject to this judgment. The Great Eraser removed this specificity: the Valley of Yehoshafat judgment is universalized into a generic 'last judgment' about individual sin. But the text is specific: the nations are judged for what they did to YHWH's people. The scattering. The land division. The identity erasure. These are the charges.
